summary: Gruber analyzes the fourth annual MDJ Power 25 list of influential figures in the Mac industry, arguing that Steve Jobs is so dominant that no one else is within an order of magnitude of his influence. He critiques the list's simple ordinal format for obscuring the vast gap between Jobs and everyone else, notes that 16 of 25 spots are held by Apple employees, and observes the near-total absence of women. Gruber shares his own ballot, which included Jobs, Jon Rubinstein, Avie Tevanian, Dave Hyatt, and Brent Simmons, defending each choice with characteristic specificity about their contributions to the Mac platform.
key_points: Jobs's influence on Apple is not merely a function of his CEO title — his micro-management and taste define every product, making him incomparably more powerful than anyone else on the list
Jobs's greatest weakness is his inability to recognize when he's wrong, exemplified by his insistence on the metallic UI theme in violation of Apple's own Human Interface Guidelines
Apple's growing dominance as both hardware maker and application developer has concentrated power within the company, with 16 of 25 slots occupied by Apple employees
The absence of women from the list reflects a real industry problem, not voter bias, and the situation has actually worsened since the 1990s when women held prominent Apple leadership positions
Gruber's ballot reveals his priorities: hardware execution (Rubinstein), OS architecture (Tevanian), web rendering technology (Hyatt), and killer Mac-only software (Simmons)
money_quotes: Anyone who doesn't rank Jobs first doesn't have enough of a clue to deserve a vote in the poll.
Jobs does not run Apple via hand-waving and delegation — he is a micro-manager with an unrivaled attention to detail.
He has impeccable taste, and an amazing gift for defining new products that people will crave.
His biggest fault is equally obvious: he is oblivious to any of his weaknesses. He may be right 95 percent of the time, but when he's wrong, he stays wrong.
In terms of power and influence, no one else is even within an order of magnitude of Jobs.
Comparing them to Jobs is like comparing Roger Ebert to Steven Spielberg.
Jobs's attention seems to be binary — it's either completely on or off.
The lack of a good, system-wide HTML renderer was one of the biggest holes in the Mac OS.
key_insight: Steve Jobs's power over the Mac industry is so absolute that the MDJ Power 25 is really a Power 1 list plus 24 others whose order barely matters.
